challenges.situation: You were thrilled with your promotion until a peer mentioned, casually, that they got the same title six months earlier. The title suddenly feels cheap.

options.text: Notice nothing about your job changed, only the comparison, and ask if you'd actually trade lives.
options.feedback: Catching the swap as it happens strips its power. The facts didn't move; the measuring stick did. Usually you don't want their life — just to not feel behind.

options.text: Ask your manager to backdate or 'correct' your level so you're not behind the peer.
options.feedback: You're chasing rank, not value. Even if it works, the wiring just locates the next person ahead — a race with no finish line.

options.text: Conclude the promotion was meaningless and let it quietly sour.
options.feedback: That's letting comparison set your worth — the Duryodhana path. You lost nothing real, yet you've surrendered the joy of something you genuinely earned.

challenges.situation: A teammate who loves his job suddenly wants to leave — not for more money, but because a former classmate just made director and 'he can't stop thinking about it.'
challenges.question: What's really pulling him toward the door?

options.text: A rational career decision based on his own goals.
options.feedback: Nothing about his own work or pay changed. The trigger was entirely someone else's title — that's not a goals-based decision.

options.text: Status comparison — the classmate's rank, not his own life, is the wound.
options.feedback: He's content until a peer pulls ahead, then it's unbearable. The other person's gain costs him nothing real, yet it costs him everything — the signature of a status hunt.
Ambition builds toward something he wants; this only burns over what someone else got. The tell is that a peer's win, not his own desire, set it off.

challenges.context: Two people feel the same sting watching a peer pull ahead.

scenarioA.label: Comparison as verdict
scenarioA.text: 'They're ahead, so I'm behind, so I'm less.' The sting sets their self-worth.

scenarioB.label: Comparison as information
scenarioB.text: 'How did they pull that off?' The sting becomes a question that reveals a path.

challenges.explanation: Same trigger, opposite results. Noticing others isn't the danger — letting the noticing decide your worth is. As information it's fuel; as a verdict it's poison.

challenges.front: Once I hit the next level, the comparison ache will finally stop.
challenges.back: There's always someone ahead, and hedonic adaptation makes each gain the new baseline within days. Comparison isn't measurement — it's a hunt that never ends. The only version you can win is against past-you, where you own both data points and the finish line.

principle.text: Comparison is not measurement — it's a status hunt, and the hunt never ends.
principle.subtext: There is always someone ahead. So when your satisfaction depends on ranking, you've signed up for a race with no finish line, where every win just reveals the next person to lose to.

reflection: Name three things you have right now that a younger you would have been thrilled to reach. When did you last actually feel their value — and what comparison stole it?
